teaching.title: Comparison Operators in Java
teaching.analogy: Comparison operators are judges in a courtroom — they look at two values and deliver a verdict: true or false. Every decision in Java starts with a comparison.
teaching.content: Java uses the same 6 comparison operators as most languages: == (equals), != (not equals), > (greater), < (less), >= (greater or equal), <= (less or equal). They always return a boolean: true or false.

examples.title: All 6 Comparison Operators
examples.code:
int a = 10, b = 5;
System.out.println(a == b);  // false
System.out.println(a != b);  // true
System.out.println(a > b);   // true
System.out.println(a < b);   // false
System.out.println(a >= 10); // true
System.out.println(b <= 4);  // false

examples.title: == vs .equals() for Strings
examples.explanation: CRITICAL: Use .equals() to compare String content in Java!

teaching.funFact: Using == to compare Strings in Java is one of the most classic Java bugs! == checks if two String variables point to the SAME object in memory, not if they have the same text. ALWAYS use .equals()! 🐛

teaching.title: Logical Operators — &&, ||, !
teaching.analogy: && is like a guard requiring BOTH passes to enter. || is like a door that opens if EITHER card works. ! is the NOT gate — it flips true to false and vice versa.
teaching.content: Java has three logical operators: && (AND — both must be true), || (OR — at least one must be true), ! (NOT — flips the boolean). Java uses && and ||, not Python's 'and'/'or'.

examples.title: Logical Operators
examples.explanation: Combine conditions to make complex decisions.

examples.title: Short-Circuit Evaluation
examples.explanation: Java stops evaluating as soon as the result is determined.

teaching.funFact: Java uses && and || (double symbol) for boolean logic. It also has & and | (single symbol) which are BITWISE operators. Don't mix them up — & evaluates both sides always, && short-circuits! ⚡

teaching.title: Arithmetic & Compound Assignment
teaching.analogy: Compound assignment operators are shortcuts: instead of 'score = score + 10', you write 'score += 10'. Java also has ++ and -- for incrementing/decrementing by 1 — common in loops.
teaching.content: Java arithmetic: +, -, *, /, % (modulo). Compound assignment: +=, -=, *=, /=, %=. Increment/decrement: ++ (add 1), -- (subtract 1). Integer division truncates: 7/2 = 3, not 3.5.

examples.title: Arithmetic & Compound Ops
examples.code:
int score = 100;
score += 50;   // score = 150
score -= 20;   // score = 130
score *= 2;    // score = 260
score /= 4;    // score = 65 (integer div)
score %= 10;   // score = 5  (remainder)

// Increment and decrement:
int i = 5;
i++;  // i = 6
i--;  // i = 5
System.out.println(i); // 5

examples.title: Integer Division vs Float
examples.explanation: Divide by double to get decimal result.

teaching.funFact: The % operator (modulo) gives the REMAINDER. 7 % 3 = 1 because 7 = 2×3 + 1. Modulo is used everywhere: even/odd check (n%2==0), clock math, cycling through items! 🔢

mcq.q: How should you compare two String values in Java?
mcq.explanation: String Equality: Use .equals() for String content comparison. == compares object references (memory addresses), not content. s1.equals(s2) is ALWAYS correct for comparing String text.

mcq.q: What is the Java logical AND operator?
mcq.explanation: Java AND: Java uses && (double ampersand) for logical AND. Python uses the word 'and'. && short-circuits — if the left side is false, the right side is never evaluated.

mcq.q: What does 10 % 3 evaluate to in Java?
mcq.explanation: Modulo: % gives the remainder. 10 ÷ 3 = 3 remainder 1. So 10 % 3 = 1. Modulo is used to check even/odd (n % 2 == 0 means even).

mcq.q: What does 7 / 2 evaluate to in Java (both ints)?
mcq.explanation: Int Division: When both operands are int, Java performs integer division — the decimal is TRUNCATED. 7/2 = 3 (not 3.5). To get 3.5, use 7.0/2 or (double)7/2.

mcq.q: What does !(5 > 3) evaluate to?
mcq.explanation: NOT Operator: 5 > 3 is true. !(true) = false. The ! operator flips/negates the boolean. !(false) = true.

coding.title: String Comparison
coding.objective: What method compares String content in Java?
coding.hint: Not ==. It's a method that returns true if both strings have the same characters.

coding.title: Compound Assignment
coding.objective: After 'int x = 10; x += 5; x *= 2;' what is x?

options: Should use .equals(): if (name.equals("Alex")) — == compares references not content
debugging.answer: Should use .equals(): if (name.equals("Alex")) — == compares references not content
debugging.hint: == on Strings compares memory addresses, not text content. Always use .equals() for String comparison.

options: int a, b can't be on same line
debugging.hint: 'and' is Python syntax. Java's logical AND is && (double ampersand).

prediction.explanation: 8 % 3 = remainder of 8÷3 = 2. 8 / 3 = 2 (integer division, truncated). Both print 2.
true && false = false. true || false = true. !true = false.

teaching.title: if / else if / else
teaching.analogy: if/else is like a traffic roundabout with multiple exits. You check the first condition — does it apply? Take that exit. If not, check the next. The final 'else' is the default exit if nothing else matches.
teaching.content: Java if/else uses curly braces {} for blocks. The condition goes in parentheses. else if allows multiple conditions. else is the fallback. Important: Java uses boolean conditions, not truthy/falsy like Python.

examples.title: Full if/else if/else
examples.explanation: Check grade and print the correct letter.

examples.title: Python vs Java if

teaching.funFact: Java requires parentheses around the condition: if (x > 5) not if x > 5. Python does the opposite — no parentheses needed, but a colon is mandatory. Both require the colon/braces to open the block! 🔑

teaching.title: Nested if & Single-line if
teaching.analogy: Nested ifs are like Russian dolls — an if inside another if. Only if the outer condition passes do you even check the inner one. It's efficient: no point checking 'is the user an admin?' if they're not even logged in!
teaching.content: You can put if statements inside other if blocks (nesting). For single-statement blocks, you can skip curly braces — but this is risky and not recommended. Always use {} for clarity.

examples.title: Nested if
examples.explanation: Inner if only runs if outer if is true.

examples.title: Single-line if (no braces)

teaching.funFact: In 2014, Apple's SSL/TLS bug (known as 'goto fail') was caused by a missing curly brace in a C if statement — leaving billions of iOS devices vulnerable to man-in-the-middle attacks. ALWAYS use {}! 🔐

mcq.q: What is Java's equivalent of Python's 'elif'?
mcq.explanation: Java elif: Java uses 'else if' (two words with a space). Python uses 'elif'. Both mean the same thing — check this condition only if all previous conditions were false.

mcq.q: In Java, what wraps the if condition?
mcq.explanation: If Syntax: Java if condition must be in parentheses: if (condition). The code block goes in curly braces {}. Python uses no parentheses but requires a colon.

mcq.q: What does the 'else' block do?
options: Runs when all conditions are true
Runs when NO previous condition was true
mcq.explanation: Else: The else block is the fallback — it runs only when NO previous if or else if condition was true. It's optional; if omitted, nothing runs when all conditions fail.

mcq.q: int x = 5; if (x > 3) System.out.println("A"); System.out.println("B"); What prints?
mcq.explanation: Brace Trap: Without {}, only the NEXT single statement is inside the if. 'B' is always printed regardless of the condition — it's outside the if block. 5 > 3, so A prints. B always prints.

mcq.q: What value does an if condition evaluate to?
mcq.explanation: Boolean Only: Unlike Python where any truthy value works, Java if conditions must evaluate to boolean (true/false). if (5) would be a compile error in Java — not like C where 0 is false!

coding.title: Java else if
coding.objective: What two keywords form Java's 'elif' equivalent?
coding.hint: Two words — Java's version of Python's 'elif'.

coding.title: Condition in Parens
coding.objective: Complete the if statement to check if score > 50.
coding.hint: Condition goes in parentheses in Java.

coding.title: Grade Check
coding.objective: int grade = 85. What does this print? if (grade>=90) 'A'; else if (grade>=80) 'B'; else 'C'
coding.hint: 85 is not >= 90, but IS >= 80. Second condition matches → B.

debugging.hint: Java requires parentheses around the if condition: if (condition) { ... }

options: = assigns (changes isRaining). Should be == or just: if (isRaining)
debugging.answer: = assigns (changes isRaining). Should be == or just: if (isRaining)
debugging.hint: = is assignment, == is comparison. But simplest fix: if (isRaining) — the boolean is already true/false.

prediction.explanation: x=15. 15>20 is false. 15>10 is true → prints 'medium'. Once a condition matches, rest are skipped.
a > 3 = true. b > 8 = true. true && true = true → if block runs → 'both' printed.

teaching.title: switch Statement
teaching.analogy: switch is like a physical switchboard with multiple channels. You dial to a specific channel (case) and it plays exactly that — unless you tell it to stop (break). Without break, it 'falls through' to the next channel automatically!
teaching.content: switch evaluates an expression and jumps to the matching case. Without break, it falls through to the next case. default is the catch-all when no case matches. Java switch works with int, char, String, and enums.

examples.title: Switch with break
examples.explanation: break stops execution after each case.

examples.title: Fall-Through & String switch
examples.explanation: Without break, execution falls through to next case.

teaching.funFact: Java 14 introduced the modern 'switch expression' with -> arrows and no fall-through: case 1 -> "Monday"; No break needed! Major companies are migrating to this cleaner syntax. 🚀

teaching.title: Ternary Operator ? :
teaching.analogy: The ternary operator is a shorthand if/else that fits on one line. It's like asking a question and getting an answer in one breath: 'Is it raining? Yes: take umbrella. No: leave it.'
teaching.content: Syntax: condition ? valueIfTrue : valueIfFalse. It's an EXPRESSION that returns a value — unlike if/else which is a statement. Use it for simple, readable one-liners.

examples.title: Ternary in Action
When NOT to use ternary
examples.explanation: Keep it simple — don't nest ternaries!

teaching.funFact: The ternary operator gets its name from having THREE operands (condition, true-value, false-value). Most operators are binary (two operands like a + b). It's the only ternary operator in Java! 🔢

mcq.q: What happens without 'break' in a switch case?
options: It falls through to the next case
mcq.explanation: Fall-Through: Without break, Java 'falls through' — it keeps executing every case below the matched one. This is a common bug source but sometimes intentional to group cases.

mcq.q: What is the switch case used when NO case matches?
mcq.explanation: Default Case: 'default' in switch is like 'else' in if/else — it runs when no case matches. It's optional but recommended to handle unexpected values.

mcq.q: What does (x > 5) ? 'yes' : 'no' evaluate to when x = 3?
mcq.explanation: Ternary: x=3. 3 > 5 is false → the FALSE side is returned: 'no'. Ternary: condition ? trueValue : falseValue.

mcq.q: Can Java switch work with String values?
mcq.explanation: String switch: Since Java 7, switch supports String values. It uses .equals() internally for comparison — so it correctly matches String content, not references.

mcq.q: int m = (8 > 5) ? 8 : 5; What is m?
mcq.explanation: Ternary: 8 > 5 is true → the TRUE value is returned: 8. m = 8.

coding.title: Switch keyword
coding.objective: What keyword starts a Java multi-case branch statement?
coding.hint: 6 letters — the keyword for multi-case branching.

coding.title: Ternary Expression
Max of Two
coding.objective: Use ternary to find max. int a=4, b=9. (a>b)?a:b = ?

options: Missing break after each case — all three will print due to fall-through
debugging.answer: Missing break after each case — all three will print due to fall-through
debugging.hint: Without break, switch falls through and executes ALL cases from the match down. Add break; after each case body.

options: Ternary returns a VALUE — wrap in System.out.println(): System.out.println(x > 3 ? "big" : "small");
debugging.answer: Ternary returns a VALUE — wrap in System.out.println(): System.out.println(x > 3 ? "big" : "small");
debugging.hint: Ternary is an expression (produces a value), not a statement. void methods like println() can't be used as ternary values.

prediction.explanation: x=2 matches case 2: prints 'B'. No break → falls through to case 3: prints 'C'. Case 3 has break → stops. Output: B↵C.
10 % 2 = 0. 0 == 0 is true → ternary returns 'even'. Output: even.

teaching.title: Block Scope in Java
teaching.analogy: In Java, variables live only inside the curly braces {} where they're declared. It's like renting a hotel room — you can use the room's contents while you're checked in, but when you leave {} they're gone. You can't take them into the hallway.
teaching.content: Java scope is determined by curly braces {}. Variables declared inside an if, loop, or method block only exist within that block. Unlike Python, even if blocks create their own scope for local variables.

examples.title: Block Scope
examples.explanation: Variable declared in {} only lives there.

examples.title: Python vs Java Scope
examples.explanation: Python if blocks DON'T create new scope. Java ones DO.

teaching.funFact: Java's strict block scoping is intentional — it prevents 'variable leakage' bugs. Python's more relaxed scoping is convenient but can cause confusion with variables accidentally persisting after loops! 🎯

teaching.title: Variable Shadowing & Best Practices
teaching.analogy: Shadowing is like a child having the same name as their parent in the same family. It causes confusion! In Java, an inner scope variable can shadow an outer scope variable with the same name — the inner one takes over within its block.
teaching.content: Shadowing occurs when an inner block declares a variable with the same name as an outer block. Java allows it in some contexts but it's confusing. Best practice: use unique, descriptive names. Don't shadow variables intentionally.

examples.title: Shadowing Example
examples.explanation: Inner 'score' shadows outer 'score' within the if block.

teaching.funFact: Java 16+ introduced 'pattern matching for instanceof' which uses block-scoped variables automatically: if (obj instanceof String s) { s.length(); } — no casting needed! 🆕

mcq.q: A variable declared inside an if block {} in Java — can you use it outside?
options: No — Java {} creates block scope, variable is destroyed after the block
mcq.explanation: Java Block Scope: Every {} creates a new scope. Variables declared inside a block only live within that block. This is different from Python where if blocks don't create new scope.

mcq.q: In what key way does Java scope differ from Python scope?
options: Java {} blocks create scope for variables; Python if blocks do NOT
mcq.explanation: Key Difference: Python if/for blocks don't create variable scope — variables declared inside remain accessible outside. Java {} blocks DO create scope — variables are block-local.

mcq.q: Where should you declare a variable you need throughout a whole method?
options: At the top of the method (method scope)
mcq.explanation: Method Scope: Declare variables at the METHOD level (outside any inner block) if you need them throughout. Declare them as close to first use as possible for clarity.

mcq.q: int x = 5; if (true) { int x = 10; } — what is the issue?
options: Variable shadowing — same name in inner scope is not allowed in all cases
mcq.explanation: Shadowing: Declaring a variable with the same name in an inner block shadows the outer one. Java may or may not allow this depending on context — but it's always confusing. Use unique names!

coding.title: Scope Check
coding.objective: Is a variable declared inside {} accessible outside? Type yes or no.
coding.hint: Java {} blocks create block scope — temp only lives inside the if block.

coding.title: Where to Declare
coding.objective: To use a variable both inside and outside an if block, declare it ___.
coding.hint: Declare it outside/before the if block with its type keyword.

options: grade is declared inside the if block — it doesn't exist outside. Declare: String grade = ""; before the if.
debugging.answer: grade is declared inside the if block — it doesn't exist outside. Declare: String grade = ""; before the if.
debugging.hint: Variables declared inside {} blocks don't exist outside them. Move the String grade declaration before the if block.

prediction.explanation: x is declared at method scope — accessible everywhere. bonus is used and added to x inside the block. After the block, x = 125. bonus is gone but x persists.

teaching.title: ⚠️ BOSS — THE DECISION ENGINE
teaching.analogy: The Decision Engine is a massively parallel processor that makes millions of decisions per second. It knows every operator, every branching path, every scope rule. To defeat it, your logic must be flawless!
teaching.content: THE DECISION ENGINE tests ALL Level 2 Java: comparison operators (== vs .equals()), logical operators (&&, ||, !), if/else if/else, switch/case/break/default, ternary operator, and Java block scope.

examples.title: Full Level 2 arsenal
examples.code:
✅ == vs .equals() for Strings
✅ &&, ||, ! operators
✅ Integer division & modulo (%)
✅ if / else if / else with ()
✅ switch / case / break / default
✅ Fall-through behavior
✅ Ternary operator ? :
✅ Java block scope with {}

teaching.funFact: The Java compiler generates bytecode that can have thousands of branch instructions (if/switch) per method. The JVM's JIT compiler optimizes the most-used branches — making Java as fast as C++ in many benchmarks! ⚡

mcq.q: String s = "hi". Which correctly checks if s equals "hi"?
mcq.explanation: .equals(): Use String.equals() for content comparison. == compares object references — may return false even for equal strings depending on how they were created.

mcq.q:
int x = 10, y = 0. Safely check if x/y > 5 without crashing:
if (y != 0 && x/y > 5) — is this safe?
options: Yes — && short-circuits, skips x/y when y==0
mcq.explanation: Short-Circuit: && evaluates left first. If y != 0 is false (y IS 0), the right side x/y is NEVER evaluated — preventing the divide-by-zero crash. This is intentional safety.

mcq.q: switch (day) { case 1: print('A'); case 2: print('B'); break; } day=1. What prints?
mcq.explanation: Fall-Through: day=1 matches case 1: prints 'A'. No break → falls through to case 2: prints 'B'. break stops there. Output: A then B.

mcq.q: int result = (5 > 3) ? 100 : 0; What is result?
mcq.explanation: Ternary: 5 > 3 is true → the TRUE value (100) is returned. result = 100.
Scope Error: A causes a compile error — 'n' is declared inside the if block and doesn't exist outside it. B is valid — 'n' is used inside the same block it was declared.

coding.title: Boss: Ternary Even/Odd
coding.objective: Use ternary to check if n=7 is even or odd. n%2==0 → 'even' else 'odd'

coding.title: Boss: Fix the String comparison
coding.objective: How should you compare String name to 'Neo'?
coding.hint: Never use == for Strings — use this method.

options: status is declared inside {} blocks — it's out of scope outside them. Declare: String status; before the if.
if and else can't both declare status
debugging.answer: status is declared inside {} blocks — it's out of scope outside them. Declare: String status; before the if.
debugging.hint: Declare String status = ""; BEFORE the if block, then assign inside. The variable must exist in the outer scope to be used after the block.

prediction.explanation: x=10. 10>=9 is true → ternary returns 'A'. The nested ternary is never evaluated.
